steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as the etiology of pancreatitis; hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis
ensemble.information_gap: The etiology of the confirmed biochemical pancreatitis (lipase 1064) is unknown — specifically whether gallstones or biliary sludge are present, and whether the biliary tree is dilated, which would point to a gallstone/obstructive cause requiring urgent intervention versus a non-biliary cause (e.g., hypertriglyceridemia, which is already documented at 208 mg/dL)
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallstones, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder wall thickening, or common bile duct dilation suggesting cholelithiasis or choledocholithiasis as the precipitant of pancreatitis; alternatively, a normal gallbladder shifting suspicion toward hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >17× the upper limit of normal confirming acute pancreatitis, RUQ/epigastric tenderness, and no prior imaging, a right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the standard first-line imaging to identify a biliary etiology — the most common and immediately actionable cause — and is directly supported by the clinical and laboratory findings visible in the workup.
grounding: History: epigastric and RUQ pain for ___ weeks
ALT: 21.0 IU/L, AST: 15.0 IU/L, Alkaline Phosphatase: 58.0 IU/L — liver enzymes normal, biliary obstruction not yet excluded
Lactate: 2.5 mmol/L (ref 0.5-2.0) — mildly elevated, suggesting some physiologic stress
History: HYPERCHOLESTEROLEMIA, stopped statin due to muscle weakness
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has biochemically confirmed acute pancreatitis (lipase >17× ULN) with RUQ/epigastric pain, and the two most common etiologies to distinguish are gallstone/biliary disease and hypertriglyceridemia (triglycerides are elevated at 208 mg/dL but not severely so). The liver enzymes and bilirubin are currently normal, which does not exclude choledocholithiasis or biliary sludge, so a bedside ultrasound of the gallbladder and biliary tree is the logical first imaging step to identify stones, ductal dilation, or cholecystitis — findings that would directly change management (e.g., ERCP, cholecystectomy planning) versus a non-biliary cause managed conservatively.

ALT: 21.0 IU/L, AST: 15.0 IU/L, Alkaline Phosphatase: 58.0 IU/L — liver enzymes normal, biliary obstruction not yet excluded

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has biochemically confirmed acute pancreatitis (lipase >17× ULN) with RUQ/epigastric pain, and the two most common etiologies to distinguish are gallstone/biliary disease and hypertriglyceridemia (triglycerides are elevated at 208 mg/dL but not severely so). verification.actual_finding: No gallstones, no gallbladder wall thickening, no biliary dilation (CBD 4 mm, no intrahepatic ductal dilation), and a normal gallbladder.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The report delivered exactly the 'alternatively normal gallbladder' outcome the physician anticipated, shifting suspicion away from a biliary/obstructive etiology and toward hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis.
